html:
Axtel is not the net-cash, asset-light story in the prior draft. The better thesis is more interesting: it is a levered Mexican digital-infrastructure and enterprise IT stub where small improvements in EBITDA, refinancing cost, and market multiple create outsized equity torque.

At roughly MXN 2.70 per CPO, Axtel’s market cap is about MXN 7.5B. Against 1Q26 net debt of US$481M, or roughly MXN 8.7B, EV is about MXN 16.2B. Management’s 2026 guide is MXN 12.85B revenue and MXN 3.8B EBITDA, so the stock trades near 4.3x current-year EBITDA. That is the core mispricing.

The market still sees old Axtel: a low-priced Mexican telco CPO with leverage, weak liquidity, and a messy ALFA/Controladora overhang. The actual business is now Alestra enterprise services plus Axnet digital infrastructure. Axtel says it has more than 55,000 km of fiber, presence in more than 90% of Mexico’s industrial parks, and is repositioning Axnet as a neutral high-capacity bridge for carriers, cloud, data centers, and content platforms. That matters because nearshoring, AI traffic, cybersecurity, cloud migration, and cross-border industrial connectivity are not marginal themes for Mexico. They are the demand curve.

The latest quarter looked ugly on the surface, which is why the opportunity exists. 1Q26 comparable EBITDA fell 31% to MXN 739M, and reported EBITDA was hit by a MXN 347M reorganization charge. Net income was a MXN 270M loss. But underneath that, enterprise revenue grew 6%, enterprise IT and cybersecurity grew 16%, managed networks grew 45%, government grew 8%, and legacy enterprise voice was only 6% of enterprise revenue. Wholesale was down 32% because of a tough one-time comp from 1Q25, not because the whole platform broke. Management also reduced headcount by 11%, with savings starting in 2Q26, and March showed a pickup in new contract acquisition.

The most important variant point is that Axtel has a lot more operating and financial torque than a normal 4x EBITDA stock. Net debt is larger than the market cap, so every 1.0x turn of EBITDA multiple on MXN 3.8B of guided EBITDA is worth about MXN 1.37 per CPO, or roughly 50% of the current share price. If EBITDA moves from MXN 3.8B to MXN 4.3B and the multiple moves from 4.3x to 5.5x, the equity almost doubles. That is not heroic. It only requires the market to treat Axtel as a credible infrastructure and enterprise IT cash-flow compounder rather than a structurally declining telco.

There are real proof points. In 2025, revenue rose 7%, EBITDA rose 3%, operating income rose 25%, and the company generated about US$80M of free cash flow while prepaying US$55M of syndicated bank debt. Since December 2024, total prepayments reached about US$90M, saving roughly US$7M of annual interest. Fitch upgraded Axtel to BB with a stable outlook. The company also refinanced into more peso debt, reducing FX exposure.

The strategic news is better than the stock reaction. Axtel signed a 12-year agreement to operate Tecnológico de Monterrey’s Uptime Tier III data center in Monterrey, adding colocation capacity with a marquee anchor customer. It moved its McAllen presence to MDC’s MCA2 interconnection hub, making the U.S.-Mexico digital corridor more investable. Axtel also partnered with SimplyAsk.ai so Alestra can resell AI workflow automation across more than 300 enterprise accounts. None of these is large enough alone to justify the stock, but together they change the label from “Mexican telecom debt story” to “nearshoring data transport, managed network, cybersecurity, AI-services reseller, and colocation option.”

Free cash flow is the valuation anchor. Guided 2026 EBITDA of MXN 3.8B less roughly MXN 1.55B capex and around MXN 0.8B to MXN 0.9B cash interest leaves roughly MXN 1.3B to MXN 1.5B of normalized equity FCF before working-capital noise. That is an 17% to 20% FCF yield on the current market cap. It is not net cash cheapness. It is levered FCF cheapness, and the debt paydown is part of the equity story.

The shareholder return signal also improved. In March 2026, shareholders approved the first cash dividend in company history, MXN 0.0623 per CPO, and a MXN 100M buyback authorization. The dividend is only about a 2.3% yield, but it breaks the old pattern of minority shareholders waiting forever while creditors got all the cash flow. More important, buybacks at 4x forward EBITDA are highly accretive if management actually uses the authorization.

The bear case is not imaginary. Axtel is leveraged, 1Q26 was weak, Mexico macro can slow enterprise IT budgets, government contracts are lumpy, and Controladora Axtel controls 54.9% of the equity. Minority shareholders have limited control. The company also sold its old mass-market fiber and data-center assets years ago, so this is not a pure data-center REIT or hyperscale landlord. It is a network and managed-services operator with some colocation exposure. That deserves a discount.

But the discount is too large. Axtel does not need a heroic 10x infrastructure multiple to work. It needs to prove that 2026 guidance is intact, that the 11% workforce action lifts margins from 2Q onward, and that the McAllen/Tec/AI initiatives show up as higher-quality revenue. If that happens, the equity can rerate before reported net income looks clean, because EBITDA and FCF will already be doing the work.

The cleanest way to own the thesis is as a 12 to 24 month deleveraging and relabeling trade. The market is still anchored to a low-liquidity CPO, one neutral analyst target around MXN 3.20, and retail confusion around AXTELCPO versus Controladora Axtel. That is exactly the setup where a small cap can move from ignored to obvious only after the numbers force it.

Bear case target: MXN 1.50. EBITDA disappoints to MXN 3.1B, leverage stays elevated, and the market applies only 4.0x EBITDA. Implied EV is MXN 12.4B; after net debt, equity value is near MXN 3.8B, or about MXN 1.35 per CPO, rounded to MXN 1.50. Downside from MXN 2.70 is 44%.

Base case target: MXN 5.25. EBITDA reaches MXN 4.0B as cost savings and enterprise growth offset wholesale legacy pressure, net debt falls toward MXN 8.0B, and the market assigns 5.6x EBITDA. Equity value is about MXN 14.4B, or MXN 5.20 per CPO. Upside is 94%.

Bull case target: MXN 8.25. Axtel proves that Axnet cross-border capacity, enterprise cybersecurity, managed networks, and colocation are durable growth assets. EBITDA reaches MXN 4.5B, net debt falls toward MXN 7.0B, and the market pays 6.7x EBITDA, still below premium infrastructure peers. Equity value is roughly MXN 23B, or MXN 8.25 per CPO. Upside is 206%.
